chunk_summary: Management of oversupply and forceful let-down focuses on reducing milk flow rate, improving infant feeding control, and using techniques like block feeding and laid-back positions to alleviate symptoms and prevent complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the symptoms and challenges of oversupply and forceful let-down in breastfeeding.
Learn effective positioning techniques to manage milk flow and improve infant feeding control.
Explore block feeding as a strategy to reduce milk production and prevent oversupply complications.
Recognize when to reassess latch or consider alternative causes of feeding distress.
key_facts: Oversupply can cause maternal breast fullness, recurrent plugged ducts, mastitis, and infant feeding distress such as choking, coughing, or frothy stools.
Block feeding involves offering the same breast for 2–3 hours to reduce milk production via local feedback inhibition.
Avoid pumping 'to empty' unless medically indicated, as it perpetuates oversupply.
Laid-back or side-lying positions help slow milk flow and improve infant feeding control.
Pharmacologic suppression of milk supply is rarely needed and should be specialist-directed.
reference_quote: Initial management focuses on reducing flow rate and improving infant control. Use laid-back or side-lying positions so gravity slows milk flow, and allow the infant to pause frequently. (ABM Clinical Protocol #32)
document_key_topics: Symptoms and complications of oversupply and forceful let-down
Techniques for managing milk flow and infant feeding distress
Block feeding as a strategy for reducing milk production
When to reassess latch or consider alternative causes of feeding issues
